title: Emergency Guide: Cat Dragging Back Legs and Vocalizing in Pain
meta_desc: If your cat is dragging their back legs and in pain, they need immediate emergency care. Learn about Saddle Thrombus and why every second counts.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden loss of use in a cat's back legs is a life-threatening medical emergency known as a Saddle Thrombus that requires immediate veterinary intervention.

What is a Saddle Thrombus in cats?

When a cat suddenly loses the use of their back legs and is in visible distress, it is most often caused by a Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), commonly referred to as a 'Saddle Thrombus.' This condition occurs when a blood clot, often originating from the heart, travels through the aorta and becomes lodged at the junction where the blood supply splits to the hind legs. This blockages cuts off circulation, leading to sudden paralysis and intense pain.

Is sudden back leg paralysis in my cat a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a critical medical emergency with a high urgency level.
  • Seek immediate veterinary care at an emergency 24-hour clinic without delay.
  • This condition is life-threatening and requires professional intervention to manage pain and attempt to restore blood flow.

Why should I take a video or photo of my cat during this emergency?

  • If it is safe to do so without delaying your departure, take a quick 5-second video of your cat's movement.
  • Capture a clear photo of their paw pads to check for 'cyanosis' (a blue or pale tint).
  • These visuals help the veterinary team confirm a diagnosis and prioritize your cat for immediate stabilization upon arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Arterial thromboembolism, often secondary to conditions causing left atrial enlargement such as cardiomyopathy, hyperthyroidism, or congenital heart disease, can result in acute hindlimb pain, paresis, or paralysis in cats. Clinical signs include vocalization due to extreme pain, decreased or absent femoral or pedal pulses, and cool, swollen gastrocnemius muscles. While some cats may experience spontaneous clot lysis, numerous treatments have proven largely unsuccessful compared to allowing the thromboembolus to resolve on its own. Doppler ultrasonography can help evaluate blood flow in the distal aorta and femoral arteries, and ultrasound can identify the thromboembolus. Management consists of analgesics, nursing care, and treatment of any underlying disease. Anticoagulants, such as unfractionated heparin or low-molecular-weight heparin, are used to reduce continued thrombus formation. Neurologic deficits may improve within 2-3 weeks, but complete recovery can take up to 6 months, and permanent deficits are possible.

Chapter: Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1245)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications like Tylenol or Aspirin, as these are highly toxic and can be fatal. Only a veterinarian can safely administer the necessary pain relief for this condition.

faqs.question: Are there other causes for back leg paralysis?
faqs.answer: While Saddle Thrombus is the most common cause of sudden hind limb paralysis in cats, other possibilities include spinal trauma or severe neurological issues. Regardless of the cause, sudden paralysis is always an emergency.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat as calm as possible in a secure carrier lined with soft blankets. Do not attempt to massage the legs, as this can be extremely painful for the cat.
